([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Casa Savoia*, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Maison de Savoie*, [Arpitan](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al "Franco-Provençal"): *Mêson de Savouè*) is a [royal house](/wiki/Royal_house "Royal house") (formally a [dynasty](/wiki/Dynasty "Dynasty")) that was established in 1003 in the historical region of [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy"), which was originally part of the [Kingdom of Burgundy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Burgundy "Kingdom of Burgundy") and now lies mostly within southeastern France. Through gradual expansions, the family grew in power, first ruling the [County of Savoy](/wiki/County_of_Savoy "County of Savoy"), a small Alpine county northwest of Italy, and later gaining absolute rule of the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily"). During the years 1713 to 1720, they were handed the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1324%E2%80%931720) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1324–1720)") and would exercise direct rule from then onward as [Piedmont–Sardinia](/wiki/Piedmont%E2%80%93Sardinia "Piedmont–Sardinia"), which was the legal [predecessor state](/wiki/Predecessor_state "Predecessor state") of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") and the [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire"), which in turn are the predecessors of the present-day [Italian Republic](/wiki/History_of_the_Italian_Republic "History of the Italian Republic").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The **House of Savoy** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Casa Savoia*, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Maison de Savoie*, [Arpitan](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al "Franco-Provençal"): *Mêson de Savouè*) is a [royal house](/wiki/Royal_house "Royal house") (formally a [dynasty](/wiki/Dynasty "Dynasty")) that was established in 1003 in the historical region of [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy"), which was originally part of the [Kingdom of Burgundy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Burgundy "Kingdom of Burgundy") and now lies mostly within southeastern France. Through gradual expansions, the family grew in power, first ruling the [County of Savoy](/wiki/County_of_Savoy "County of Savoy"), a small Alpine county northwest of Italy, and later gaining absolute rule of the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily"). During the years 1713 to 1720, they were handed the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1324%E2%80%931720) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1324–1720)") and would exercise direct rule from then onward as [Piedmont–Sardinia](/wiki/Piedmont%E2%80%93Sardinia "Piedmont–Sardinia"), which was the legal [predecessor state](/wiki/Predecessor_state "Predecessor state") of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") and the [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire"), which in turn are the predecessors of the present-day [Italian Republic](/wiki/History_of_the_Italian_Republic "History of the Italian Republic").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From rule of a region on the French–Italian border, by the time of the [abolition of monarchy](/wiki/Abolition_of_monarchy "Abolition of monarchy") in Italy, the dynasty's realm grew to include nearly all of the [Italian peninsula](/wiki/Italian_peninsula "Italian peninsula"). Through its junior branch of [Savoy-Carignano](/wiki/Savoy-Carignano "Savoy-Carignano"), the House of Savoy led the [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") in 1861, and ruled the Kingdom of Italy until 1946. They also briefly ruled the [Kingdom of Spain](/wiki/History_of_Spain_(1808%E2%80%931874) "History of Spain (1808–1874)") during the 19th century. The Savoyard kings of Italy were [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II"), [Umberto I](/wiki/Umberto_I "Umberto I"), [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III "Victor Emmanuel III"), and [Umberto II](/wiki/Umberto_II "Umberto II"). Umberto II reigned for only a few weeks, as the last [king of Italy](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy"), before being deposed following the [1946 Italian institutional referendum](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum"), after which the Italian Republic was proclaimed.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Savoy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Savoy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

The name derives from the historical region of Savoy in the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") between what is now France and Italy. Over time, the House of Savoy expanded its territory and influence through judicious marriages and international diplomacy.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The house descended from [Humbert I, Count of Sabaudia](/wiki/Humbert_I,_Count_of_Sabaudia "Humbert I, Count of Sabaudia"), also known as Umberto I "Biancamano" (1003–1047 or 1048). The ancestry of Humbert is uncertain, as contemporary documents make no mention of his father. His family was traditionally believed to have come from Saxony;[[5]](#cite_note-5) more recent investigations into the 21st century pointed to the [County of Vienne](/wiki/County_of_Vienne "County of Vienne"), where both Humbert and his relatives held extensive possessions, as a more plausible origin.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Abbaye_royale_de_Hautecombe_II_-_200501.JPG/250px-Abbaye_royale_de_Hautecombe_II_-_200501.JPG)](/wiki/File:Abbaye_royale_de_Hautecombe_II_-_200501.JPG)

[Hautecombe Abbey](/wiki/Hautecombe_Abbey "Hautecombe Abbey"), where many of the dukes are buried

Although [Sabaudia](/wiki/Sabaudia_(Savoy) "Sabaudia (Savoy)") was originally a poor county, later [counts](/wiki/Count "Count") were diplomatically skilled, and gained control over strategic mountain passes in the Alps. Two of Humbert's sons were commendatory abbots at the [Abbey of St. Maurice, Agaunum](/wiki/Abbey_of_St._Maurice,_Agaunum "Abbey of St. Maurice, Agaunum"), on the river [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône") east of [Lake Geneva](/wiki/Lake_Geneva "Lake Geneva"), and [Saint Maurice](/wiki/Saint_Maurice "Saint Maurice") is still the patron of the House of Savoy. Humbert's son, [Otto of Savoy](/wiki/Otto_of_Savoy "Otto of Savoy"), succeeded to the title in 1051 after the death of his elder brother [Amadeus I of Savoy](/wiki/Amadeus_I_of_Savoy "Amadeus I of Savoy"). Otto married the Marchioness Adelaide of [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), bringing the [Marquessate of Susa](/wiki/Marquessate_of_Susa "Marquessate of Susa"), with the towns of Turin and Pinerolo, into the House of Savoy's possession.[[8]](#cite_note-8) They once had claims on the modern [canton of Vaud](/wiki/Canton_of_Vaud "Canton of Vaud"), where they occupied the [Château of Chillon](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_of_Chillon "Château of Chillon") in Switzerland; their access to it was cut by [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva") during the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), after which it was conquered by the [Canton of Bern](/wiki/Canton_of_Bern "Canton of Bern"). Meanwhile, [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont") was later joined with Sabaudia, and the name evolved into *Savoy* ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Savoia*).

### Expansion, retreat, and prosperity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Savoy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Expansion, retreat, and prosperity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Italy_1494.svg/250px-Italy_1494.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italy_1494.svg)

A map of Italy in 1494

By the time [Amadeus VIII](/wiki/Amadeus_VIII,_Duke_of_Savoy "Amadeus VIII, Duke of Savoy") came to power in the late 14th century, the House of Savoy had gone through a series of gradual territorial expansions and he was elevated by [Sigismund](/wiki/Sigismund,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Sigismund, Holy Roman Emperor"), the [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor"), to the Duke of Savoy in 1416.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 1494, [Charles VIII of France](/wiki/Charles_VIII_of_France "Charles VIII of France") passed through Savoy on his way to Italy and Naples, which initiated the [Italian War of 1494–1495](/wiki/Italian_War_of_1494%E2%80%931495 "Italian War of 1494–1495").[[10]](#cite_note-10) During the outbreak of the [Italian war of 1521–1526](/wiki/Italian_War_of_1521%E2%80%931526 "Italian War of 1521–1526"), the Holy Roman Emperor [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") stationed imperial troops in Savoy.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In 1536, Francis I of France invaded Savoy and Piedmont, taking Turin by April of that year.[[12]](#cite_note-Mallett230-231-12) [Charles III Duke of Savoy](/wiki/Charles_III,_Duke_of_Savoy "Charles III, Duke of Savoy"), fled to Vercelli.[[12]](#cite_note-Mallett230-231-12)

When [Emmanuel Philibert](/wiki/Emmanuel_Philibert,_Duke_of_Savoy "Emmanuel Philibert, Duke of Savoy") came to power in 1553, most of his family's territories were in French hands, so he offered to serve France's leading enemy the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") in the hope of recovering his lands. He served [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") as Governor of the Netherlands (then part of the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces")) from 1555 to 1559.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In this capacity, he led the Spanish invasion of northern France and won a victory in the [battle of St. Quentin](/wiki/Battle_of_St._Quentin_(1557) "Battle of St. Quentin (1557)") in 1557.[[14]](#cite_note-14) He took advantage of various squabbles in Europe to slowly regain territory from both the French and the Spanish, including the city of Turin. He moved the capital of the duchy from [Chambéry](/wiki/Chamb%C3%A9ry "Chambéry") to Turin.

The 17th century brought about economic development to the Turin area and the House of Savoy took part in and benefitted from that. [Charles Emmanuel II](/wiki/Charles_Emmanuel_II,_Duke_of_Savoy "Charles Emmanuel II, Duke of Savoy") developed the port of [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") and built a road through the Alps towards France, and through skillful political manoeuvres the territorial expansion continued. In the early 18th century during the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"), future King [Victor Amadeus II](/wiki/Victor_Amadeus_II "Victor Amadeus II") switched sides to assist the Habsburgs, and via the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") they rewarded him with large pieces of land in northeastern Italy and a Crown in Sicily. [Savoy rule over Sicily](/wiki/Savoy_rule_over_Sicily "Savoy rule over Sicily") lasted only seven years (1713–1720).

### Kingdom of Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Savoy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Kingdom of Italy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Italy_1796.svg/250px-Italy_1796.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italy_1796.svg)

A map of Italy in 1796

The crown of Sicily, the prestige of being [kings](/wiki/King "King") at last, and the wealth of [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") helped strengthen the House of Savoy further. In 1720, they were forced to exchange Sicily for the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia "Kingdom of Sardinia") as a result of the [War of the Quadruple Alliance](/wiki/War_of_the_Quadruple_Alliance "War of the Quadruple Alliance"). On the mainland, the dynasty continued its expansionist policies as well. Through advantageous alliances during the [War of the Polish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Polish_Succession "War of the Polish Succession") and [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession"), King [Charles Emmanuel III](/wiki/Charles_Emmanuel_III "Charles Emmanuel III") gained new lands at the expense of the [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire")-controlled [Duchy of Milan](/wiki/Duchy_of_Milan "Duchy of Milan").

In 1792, Piedmont–Sardinia joined the [First Coalition](/wiki/First_Coalition "First Coalition") against the [French First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"). It was beaten in 1796 by [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") and forced to conclude the disadvantageous [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1796) "Treaty of Paris (1796)"), giving the French army free passage through Piedmont. In 1798, [Barthélemy Catherine Joubert](/wiki/Barth%C3%A9lemy_Catherine_Joubert "Barthélemy Catherine Joubert") occupied Turin and forced [Charles Emmanuel IV](/wiki/Charles_Emmanuel_IV "Charles Emmanuel IV") to abdicate and leave for the island of [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia"). In 1814, the kingdom was restored and enlarged with the addition of the former [Republic of Genoa](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa") by the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Italia_1843-en.svg/250px-Italia_1843-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italia_1843-en.svg)

A map of Italy in 1843

In the meantime, [Italian nationalist](/wiki/Italian_nationalist "Italian nationalist") figures like [Giuseppe Mazzini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mazzini "Giuseppe Mazzini") were influencing popular opinion. Mazzini believed that [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") could only be achieved through a [popular uprising](/wiki/Popular_uprising "Popular uprising"). After the failure of the [Revolutions of 1848 in the Italian states](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848_in_the_Italian_states "Revolutions of 1848 in the Italian states"), the nationalists began to look to the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)") and its prime minister [Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour](/wiki/Camillo_Benso,_Count_of_Cavour "Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour") as leaders of the unification movement. In 1848, King [Charles Albert](/wiki/Charles_Albert_of_Sardinia "Charles Albert of Sardinia") conceded a constitution known as the *[Statuto Albertino](/wiki/Statuto_Albertino "Statuto Albertino")* to Piedmont–Sardinia, which remained the basis of the kingdom's legal system even after Italian unification was achieved and the Kingdom of Sardinia became the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") in 1861.

The Kingdom of Italy was the first Italian state to include the Italian peninsula since the fall of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] When [Victor Emmanuel](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II") was crowned King of Italy in 1861, his realm did not include the [Venetia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Lombardy%E2%80%93Venetia "Kingdom of Lombardy–Venetia") region (subject to Habsburg governance), [Lazio](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio") (with Rome), [Umbria](/wiki/Umbria "Umbria"), [Marche](/wiki/Marche "Marche"), and [Romagna](/wiki/Romagna "Romagna") (with the [Papal](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") town of [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna")). Yet the House of Savoy continued to rule Italy for several decades through the Italian independence wars as Italian unification proceeded and even as the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") raged on in the early 20th century.

### Massacres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Savoy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Massacres")]

In April 1655, based on perhaps false reports of resistance by the [Waldensians](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians"), a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") religious minority, to a plan to resettle them in remote mountain valleys, [Charles Emmanuel II](/wiki/Charles_Emmanuel_II,_Duke_of_Savoy "Charles Emmanuel II, Duke of Savoy") ordered their general massacre, which became known as the [Piedmontese Easter](/wiki/Piedmontese_Easter "Piedmontese Easter"). The massacre was so brutal it aroused indignation throughout Europe. [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), then ruler in England, began petitioning on behalf of the Waldensians, writing letters, raising contributions, calling a general fast in England, and threatening to send military forces to the rescue. The massacre prompted [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton")'s famous sonnet, "[On the Late Massacre in Piedmont](/wiki/On_the_Late_Massacre_in_Piedmont "On the Late Massacre in Piedmont")".

In 1898, the [Bava Beccaris massacre](/wiki/Bava_Beccaris_massacre "Bava Beccaris massacre") in Milan involved the use of cannons against unarmed protesters (including women and the elderly) during riots over the rising price of bread. King [Umberto I](/wiki/Umberto_I "Umberto I") of the House of Savoy congratulated General [Fiorenzo Bava Beccaris](/wiki/Fiorenzo_Bava_Beccaris "Fiorenzo Bava Beccaris") for the massacre and decorated him with the medal of Great Official of Savoy Military Order, greatly outraging a large part of the public opinion. As a result, Umberto I was assassinated in July 1900 in [Monza](/wiki/Monza "Monza") by [Gaetano Bresci](/wiki/Gaetano_Bresci "Gaetano Bresci"), the brother of one of the women massacred in the crowd, who had traveled back to Italy from the United States for the assassination. The king had previously been the target of failed assassination attempts by anarchists [Giovanni Passannante](/wiki/Giovanni_Passannante "Giovanni Passannante") and [Pietro Acciarito](/wiki/Pietro_Acciarito "Pietro Acciarito").

### Fascism and end of monarchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Savoy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Fascism and end of monarchy")]

When the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") ended, the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") fell short of what had been promised in the [London Pact](/wiki/London_Pact "London Pact") to Italy. As the economic conditions in Italy worsened after the war, popular resentment and along with it the seeds of [Italian fascism](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism") began to grow and resulted in the [March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome") by [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"). General [Pietro Badoglio](/wiki/Pietro_Badoglio "Pietro Badoglio") advised King [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III "Victor Emmanuel III") that he could easily sweep Mussolini and his rag-tag [Blackshirt](/wiki/Blackshirt "Blackshirt") army to one side but Victor Emmanuel decided to tolerate Mussolini and appointed him as [prime minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Italy "Prime minister of Italy") on 28 October 1922. The king remained silent as Mussolini engaged in one abuse of power after another from 1924 onward, and did not intervene in 1925–1926 when Mussolini dropped all pretense of democracy. By the end of 1928, the king's right to remove Mussolini from office was, at least theoretically, the only check on his power. Later, the king's failure, in the face of mounting evidence, to move against the Mussolini regime's abuses of power led to much criticism and had dire future consequences for Italy and for the [monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy "Monarchy") itself.

After their invasion in 1935, Italy conquered Ethiopia in the [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War") and Victor Emmanuel was crowned as Emperor of Ethiopia. He also added the Albanian crown 1939 but lost Ethiopia as part of the [East African campaign](/wiki/East_African_campaign_(World_War_II) "East African campaign (World War II)") in 1941; however, as Mussolini and the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") failed in the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") in 1943, several members of the Italian court began putting out feelers to the [Allies of World War II](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"), who in turn let it be known that Mussolini had to go. After Mussolini received a vote of no confidence from the [Fascist Grand Council](/wiki/Fascist_Grand_Council "Fascist Grand Council") on 24 July, Victor Emmanuel dismissed him from office, relinquished the Ethiopian and Albanian crowns, and appointed Pietro Badoglio as prime minister. On 8 September, the new government announced it had signed an armistice with the Allies five days earlier; however, Victor Emmanuel made another blunder when he and his government fled south to [Brindisi](/wiki/Brindisi "Brindisi"), leaving his army without orders.

As the Allies and the [Italian Resistance](/wiki/Italian_Resistance "Italian Resistance") gradually chased the [Nazis](/wiki/Nazis "Nazis") and fascists off the peninsula, it became apparent that Victor Emmanuel was too tainted by his earlier support of Mussolini to have any postwar role. Accordingly, Victor Emmanuel transferred most of his powers to his son, Crown Prince Umberto, in April 1944. Rome was liberated two months later, and Victor Emmanuel transferred his remaining powers to Umberto and named him [Lieutenant General of the Realm](/wiki/Lieutenant_General_of_the_Realm "Lieutenant General of the Realm"). Within a year, public opinion pushed for a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") to decide between retaining the monarchy or becoming a [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic"). On 9 May 1946, in a last-ditch attempt to save the monarchy, Victor Emmanuel formally abdicated in favour of his son, who became [Umberto II](/wiki/Umberto_II "Umberto II"). It did not work as the [1946 Italian institutional referendum](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum") was won by republicans with 54% of the vote. Victor Emmanuel went into exile in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), dying there a year later.

On 12 June 1946, the Kingdom of Italy formally came to an end as Umberto II transferred his powers to the prime minister [Alcide de Gasperi](/wiki/Alcide_de_Gasperi "Alcide de Gasperi") and called for the Italian people to support the new republic. He then went into exile in [Portugal](/wiki/Estado_Novo_(Portugal) "Estado Novo (Portugal)"), never to return; he died in 1983. The [Constitution of the Italian Republic](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Italian_Republic "Constitution of the Italian Republic") includes the [entrenched clause](/wiki/Entrenched_clause "Entrenched clause") that the republican form of government cannot be changed by [constitutional amendment](/wiki/Constitutional_amendment "Constitutional amendment"), thus forbidding any attempt to restore the monarchy short of adoption of an entirely new [constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution"). The Constitution of Italy also forbade male descendants of the House of Savoy from entering Italy.[[15]](#cite_note-15) This provision was removed in 2002;[[16]](#cite_note-16) as part of the deal to be allowed back into Italy, [Vittorio Emanuele](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel,_Prince_of_Naples "Victor Emmanuel, Prince of Naples"), the last claimant to the House of Savoy, renounced all claims to the throne.[[17]](#cite_note-17) He died in 2024.[[18]](#cite_note-Reuters_2024-18)

### Controversies, damage claims against Italy, and internal disputes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Savoy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Controversies, damage claims against Italy, and internal disputes")]

The [Residences of the Royal House of Savoy](/wiki/Residences_of_the_Royal_House_of_Savoy "Residences of the Royal House of Savoy") in Turin and the neighbourhood are protected as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). Although the titles and distinctions of the Italian royal family are not legally recognised by the Italian Republic, the remaining members of the House of Savoy, like dynasties of other abolished monarchies, still use some of the various titles they acquired over the millennium of their reign prior to the republic's establishment, including Duke of Savoy; Prince of Naples, previously conferred by [Joseph Bonaparte](/wiki/Joseph_Bonaparte "Joseph Bonaparte") to be hereditary on his children and grandchildren; [Prince of Piedmont](/wiki/Prince_of_Piedmont "Prince of Piedmont"); and [Duke of Aosta](/wiki/Duke_of_Aosta "Duke of Aosta").

Previously, the leadership of the House of Savoy was contested by two cousins: [Vittorio Emanuele, Prince of Naples](/wiki/Vittorio_Emanuele,_Prince_of_Naples "Vittorio Emanuele, Prince of Naples"), who used to claim the title of King of Italy, and [Prince Amedeo, Duke of Aosta](/wiki/Prince_Amedeo,_Duke_of_Aosta_(1943%E2%80%932021) "Prince Amedeo, Duke of Aosta (1943–2021)"), who claimed the title of Duke of Savoy. Supporters of Vittorio Emanuele and his descendents are known as Legitimists while supporters of Amedeo and his descendents are known as Aostaists. Their rivalry was not always peaceful. On 21 May 2004, following a dinner held by King [Juan Carlos I](/wiki/Juan_Carlos_I "Juan Carlos I") of Spain on the eve of the wedding of his son [Felipe, Prince of Asturias](/wiki/Felipe,_Prince_of_Asturias "Felipe, Prince of Asturias"), Vittorio Emanuele punched Amedeo twice in the face.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 1969, Vittorio Emanuele made his unilateral declaration of kingship, arguing that by agreeing to submit to a referendum on his place as head of state, his father (Umberto II) had thereby abdicated. Vittorio Emanuele took this action after his father allegedly called for Amedeo to visit him in Portugal to name him his heir. Under his self-assumed powers as King of Italy, Vittorio Emanuele conferred the title of Duchess of Sant'Anna di Valdieri on his then-fiancée [Marina Doria](/wiki/Marina_Doria "Marina Doria").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) In 1983, after the death of his father, with the support of the [Italian Freemasonry](/wiki/Italian_Freemasonry "Italian Freemasonry") (like many personalities of the Italian ruling class who promoted the repeal of the transitional provisions and the return to Italy, he was registered with [Licio Gelli](/wiki/Licio_Gelli "Licio Gelli")'s Masonic lodge [Propaganda Due](/wiki/Propaganda_Due "Propaganda Due") with membership number 1621),[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) as well as scattered pieces of the Italian Monarchist Party, Vittorio Emanuele proclaimed himself King of Italy as Vittorio Emanuele IV and became the historical and political reference of the House of Savoy.[[26]](#cite_note-Turrini_2024-26) In 2002, statements were published in which he accepted the end of the monarchy,[[27]](#cite_note-27) and both Vittorio Emanuele and his son [Emanuele Filiberto of Savoy](/wiki/Emanuele_Filiberto,_Prince_of_Venice_and_Piedmont "Emanuele Filiberto, Prince of Venice and Piedmont") swore loyalty to the Italian Republic and its president.[[28]](#cite_note-Corriere_della_Sera_2006-28)

In 1997, Vittorio Emanuele said on [TG2](/wiki/TG2 "TG2") that the [antisemitic](/wiki/Antisemitic "Antisemitic") laws passed under Mussolini's regime were "not so bad".[[29]](#cite_note-Arie_2003-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) Vittorio Emanuele reconsidered his words the day after and spoke of a "grave mistake",[[31]](#cite_note-31) but he did not apologize.[[32]](#cite_note-32) In 2002, with a statement issued from Geneva on the same day the [Italian racial laws](/wiki/Italian_racial_laws "Italian racial laws") of 1938 had been given [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") by the king,[[33]](#cite_note-Del_Frate_2021-33) for the first time in the history of the House of Savoy, Vittorio Emanuele officially distanced himself from the antisemitic laws,[[28]](#cite_note-Corriere_della_Sera_2006-28) and since then had tried to repair the damage by saying that the antisemitic laws had left "an indelible stain" and were the "darkest chapter" in his family's history.[[29]](#cite_note-Arie_2003-29) The 2002 statement read: "The date of 10 November, which until now represented for us the memory of an indelible stain on the history of the family, now constitutes, by a singular twist of fate, a new, fundamental stage towards the long-awaited return to the Homeland."[[33]](#cite_note-Del_Frate_2021-33) When the Savoys returned to Italy in 2003, they were met with complaints that Vittorio Emanuele and his family had made no attempt at reconciliation with the Jewish community, which was not satisfied and continued to demand a "clear sign that they have rejected that period of history".[[29]](#cite_note-Arie_2003-29) In an interview with the *[Corriere della Sera](/wiki/Corriere_della_Sera "Corriere della Sera")*, the [Union of Italian Jewish Communities](/wiki/Union_of_Italian_Jewish_Communities "Union of Italian Jewish Communities") president [Amos Luzzatto](/wiki/Amos_Luzzatto "Amos Luzzatto") stated: "I'm not saying it was he who signed the racial laws in 1938. But, as a Savoy heir, [Vittorio Emanuele] has never distanced himself from them."[[34]](#cite_note-34) On 27 January 2005, in a letter published by the *Corriere della Sera*, Vittorio Emanuele issued an apology to Italy's Jewish population, asking forgiveness from the [Italian Jewish](/wiki/Italian_Jewish "Italian Jewish") community, and declaring that it was an error for the [Italian royal family](/wiki/Italian_royal_family "Italian royal family") to have signed the racial laws of 1938.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

On 20 September 2018, during a celebration for the 100th anniversary of the Great War, Emanuele Filiberto stated: "The racial laws are a disgrace for Italy, for what happened."[[33]](#cite_note-Del_Frate_2021-33) This was seen as a timid admission of guilt and was criticised by the local Jewish community. *Shalom*, the organ of the Jewish Community of Rome, commented: "We don't like the words of Emanuele Filiberto of Savoy because they are ambiguous... and they want to water down responsibility. The racial laws are first and foremost a disgrace—not for Italy—but first and foremost for the House of Savoy which allowed without batting an eyelid—just to give an example—that dozens of Jewish officers who had served during the First World War, and who had been awarded Medals of Military Valor and who held positions of great responsibility, were expelled from the Royal Army."[[33]](#cite_note-Del_Frate_2021-33) In January 2021, ahead of [Holocaust Memorial Day](/wiki/Holocaust_Memorial_Day "Holocaust Memorial Day") in Italy, Emanuele Filiberto wrote a letter to Italy's Jewish community in which he said his family's role in rubber-stamping Mussolini's antisemitic laws caused "a wound still open for the whole of Italy", that he and his relatives "dissociate ourselves firmly" from Victor Emmanuel III who approved Mussolini's rise to power and gave the antisemitic laws royal assent, and asked for forgiveness for the king's actions. The letter was dismissed by historians as "too little too late", and was criticised by Jewish groups who condemned the family's lengthy reluctance to acknowledge its role in [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) In the letter, Emanuele Filiberto stated: "I condemn the 1938 racial laws, all of whose weight I still feel on my shoulders to this day, and with me the whole royal house ... they are a disgrace for the entire Royal House of Savoy. ... We firmly dissociate ourselves, an unacceptable document, a wound still open for the entire country."[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) Emanuele Filiberto told [TG5](/wiki/TG5 "TG5") that he was writing with "an open heart" a difficult letter whose contents "may surprise you and that perhaps you did not expect", and the time had come "once and for all" to "come to terms with the history and the past" of the Savoy family.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Some of the activities, actions, and accusations of members of the House of Savoy, especially those of Vittorio Emanuele and his legal troubles,[[26]](#cite_note-Turrini_2024-26)[[41]](#cite_note-La_Stampa_2024-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) evoked media coverage disappointing to [Italian royalists](/wiki/Italian_royalists "Italian royalists"), including among its family members.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) Vittorio Emanuele was a business intermediary on behalf of [Agusta](/wiki/Agusta "Agusta") and thanks to his friendship with [Mohammad Reza Pahlavi](/wiki/Mohammad_Reza_Pahlavi "Mohammad Reza Pahlavi") concluded sales of helicopters between Italy, Iran, and some Arab countries. In the 1970s, he was investigated in [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice") and [Trento](/wiki/Trento "Trento") for international arms trafficking to some Middle Eastern countries under embargo. This case was later transferred to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") but was later closed.[[28]](#cite_note-Corriere_della_Sera_2006-28) Most notably, in November 1991, after thirteen years of legal proceedings, the Paris [Assize Court](/wiki/Assize_Court "Assize Court") acquitted Vittorio Emanuele of the fatal wounding and [unintentional homicide](/wiki/Unintentional_homicide "Unintentional homicide") in August 1978 of [Dirk Hamer](/wiki/Dirk_Hamer "Dirk Hamer") (who was shot and killed while asleep on a yacht off [Cavallo](/wiki/Cavallo_(island) "Cavallo (island)")),[[45]](#cite_note-45) finding him guilty of unauthorised possession of a firearm during the incident;[[41]](#cite_note-La_Stampa_2024-41) this proved to be his one and only conviction of all his legal troubles.[[41]](#cite_note-La_Stampa_2024-41)

On 16 June 2006, Vittorio Emanuele was arrested in [Varenna](/wiki/Varenna "Varenna"), as investigators said he had contacts with the Mafia,[[46]](#cite_note-46) and imprisoned in [Potenza](/wiki/Potenza "Potenza") on charges of [political corruption](/wiki/Political_corruption "Political corruption") and recruitment of prostitutes for clients of the [Casinò di Campione](/wiki/Casin%C3%B2_di_Campione "Casinò di Campione") of [Campione d'Italia](/wiki/Campione_d%27Italia "Campione d'Italia"),[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) from which it emerged the *Vallettopoli* scandal.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) After seven days in jail,[[52]](#cite_note-52) Vittorio Emanuele was released and placed under house arrest instead; both Vittorio Emanuele and his family denied any wrongdoings.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) He was released from house arrest on 20 July but was required to remain within the territory of the Italian Republic; he was eventually acquitted of all charges,[[56]](#cite_note-56) including that of [criminal association](/wiki/Criminal_association "Criminal association") aimed at corruption,[[57]](#cite_note-57) gambling,[[58]](#cite_note-58) forgery ("against public administration, public faith, and property"),[[59]](#cite_note-59) exploitation of prostitution,[[60]](#cite_note-60) and aiding and abetting,[[61]](#cite_note-61) in the Savoiagate trial,[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) and in February 2015 obtained €40,000 in damages for his time in jail.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

When incarcerated in June 2006, Vittorio Emanuele was recorded admitting,[[65]](#cite_note-65) with regard to the killing of Hamer, that "I was in the wrong, but I put one over on those French judges",[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) leading to a call from Hamer's sister Birgit for Vittorio Emanuele to be retried in Italy for the killing;[[68]](#cite_note-68) in response to this and his father's arrest and charges in 2006, Emanuele Filiberto distanced himself from Vittorio Emanuele, saying that he does not share or support everything his father did,[[69]](#cite_note-69) while Amedeo of Savoy stated that Vittorio Emanuele's claims to the Headship of House Savoy were "in presence of facts that can injure the Royal House".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After a long legal fight, Birgit Hamer obtained the full video.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) The story was broken in the press by *[Il Fatto Quotidiano](/wiki/Il_Fatto_Quotidiano "Il Fatto Quotidiano")* with an article by aristocratic journalist [Beatrice Borromeo](/wiki/Beatrice_Borromeo "Beatrice Borromeo"), who also wrote the preface for a book on the murder *Delitto senza castigo* by Birgit Hamer.[[72]](#cite_note-¡Hola!_2015-72) Vittorio Emanuele sued the newspaper for defamation, claiming the video had been manipulated.[[73]](#cite_note-73) In March 2015, a court judgement ruled in favour of *Il Fatto Quotidiano*.[[72]](#cite_note-¡Hola!_2015-72) In August 2017, Italy's [Supreme Court of Cassation](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Cassation_(Italy) "Supreme Court of Cassation (Italy)") acquitted *[La Repubblica](/wiki/La_Repubblica "La Repubblica")* journalists Maurizio Crosetti and [Ezio Mauro](/wiki/Ezio_Mauro "Ezio Mauro") of a defamation lawsuit brought by Vittorio Emanuele, who had been referred to by Crosetti and Mauro as "the one who used" with ease the rifle at Cavallo, "killing a man". Crosetti and Mauro, who was also sued for omitted control due to being the then editor-in-chief, had been convicted in the first-instance trial but were acquitted on appeal, a sentence that the Supreme Court of Cassation confirmed.[[74]](#cite_note-74) In the summer of 2023, *The Prince*, a docuseries directed by Borromeo and focused on the death of Hamer, was released on [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix").[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) The docuseries concluded with Vittorio Emanuele declaring that he had no regrets and that he would do everything he had done in his life again "except Cavallu", referencing the events at Cavallo.[[26]](#cite_note-Turrini_2024-26)

In late 2007, lawyers representing Vittorio Emanuele and his son Emanuele Filiberto wrote a seven-page letter to then Italian president [Giorgio Napolitano](/wiki/Giorgio_Napolitano "Giorgio Napolitano") and Italian prime minister [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi") seeking damages for their years in exile (€260 million without considering [interest](/wiki/Interest "Interest")) and the return of their seized property after the war.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) This was confirmed during an interview on the [Rai 3](/wiki/Rai_3 "Rai 3") popular affairs programme *Ballarò*, where Emanuele Filiberto also stated that the seized property including Roman landmarks, such as the [Quirinale](/wiki/Quirinale "Quirinale") palace and [Villa Ada](/wiki/Villa_Ada "Villa Ada"), should be returned to the Savoy family.[[80]](#cite_note-80) The Italian prime minister's office released a statement stating that the Savoys are not owed any damages and suggesting that Italy may demand damages from the Savoys for their collusion with Mussolini and its wartime conduct.[[81]](#cite_note-81) The Italian Constitution contains a clause stripping the Savoys of their wealth on exile. Emanuele Filiberto acknowledged that his fiancée, whose pregnancy was revealed at the time of the couple's engagement, belonged to a more leftist milieu than his own, a fact that initially displeased his father.[[82]](#cite_note-82) On 17 December 2017, the body of Victor Emmanuel III returned to Italy to be buried at the [Sanctuary of Vicoforte](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Vicoforte "Sanctuary of Vicoforte") in Piedmont.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Judicially separated since 1976, civilly divorced in 1982, and their marriage religiously annulled in 1987, Amedeo of Aosta's first wife, [Princess Claude of Orléans](/wiki/Princess_Claude_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Princess Claude of Orléans"), revealed that she was aware that her husband fathered a child by another woman during their marriage.[[85]](#cite_note-85) Aosta acknowledged paternity of another child, born out-of-wedlock in 2006 during his second marriage but agreed to contribute financially to the child's care only after being directed to do so by court order.[[86]](#cite_note-86) The [patrilineal](/wiki/Patrilineal "Patrilineal") lineage of the House of Savoy was reduced to four males between 1996 and 2009. In 2008, [Aimone of Savoy-Aosta](/wiki/Aimone_di_Savoia_Aosta_(born_1967) "Aimone di Savoia Aosta (born 1967)") married [Princess Olga Isabelle of Greece](/wiki/Princess_Olga_Isabelle_of_Greece "Princess Olga Isabelle of Greece"), his second cousin, and they became the parents of sons Umberto and Amedeo, who were born respectively in 2009 and 2011. In 2019, Vittorio Emanuele issued a formal decree that modified the medieval law restricting succession to male heirs to place his granddaughter, [Vittoria Cristina Chiara Adelaide Marie](/wiki/Vittoria_Cristina_Chiara_Adelaide_Marie "Vittoria Cristina Chiara Adelaide Marie"), in the line of succession. Prince Aimone declared the change illegitimate, meaning the title would remain in male succession and transfer to the Savoy-Aosta branch led by Aimone.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) As of 2022, the House of Savoy was in the process of trying to reclaim family jewels that have been owned by the Italian government since the abolition of the monarchy.[[89]](#cite_note-89) Vittorio Emanuele died in February 2024,[[18]](#cite_note-Reuters_2024-18) and the House of Savoy announced: "Surrounded by his family, he died peacefully in Geneva."[[41]](#cite_note-La_Stampa_2024-41)

## Orders of knighthood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Savoy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Orders of knighthood")]

The House of Savoy has held two [dynastic orders](/wiki/Dynastic_order "Dynastic order") since 1362,[[90]](#cite_note-ordinidinasticicasasavoia.it-90) which were brought into the Kingdom of Italy as national orders. Although the kingdom ceased to exist in 1946, King Umberto II did not abdicate his role as *[fons honorum](/wiki/Fons_honorum "Fons honorum")* over the two dynastic orders over which the family has long held sovereignty and grand mastership. In the 21st century, following the dispute, both Prince Emanuele Filiberto and Prince Aimone claim to be hereditary Sovereign and Grand Master of the following orders of the House of Savoy:

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Order_of_the_Most_Holy_Annunciation_BAR.svg/120px-Order_of_the_Most_Holy_Annunciation_BAR.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Order_of_the_Most_Holy_Annunciation_BAR.svg) [Supreme Order of the Most Holy Annunciation](/wiki/Supreme_Order_of_the_Most_Holy_Annunciation "Supreme Order of the Most Holy Annunciation"), founded in 1362.[[90]](#cite_note-ordinidinasticicasasavoia.it-90)
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Cavaliere_SSML_BAR.svg/120px-Cavaliere_SSML_BAR.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cavaliere_SSML_BAR.svg) [Order of Saints Maurice and Lazarus](/wiki/Order_of_Saints_Maurice_and_Lazarus "Order of Saints Maurice and Lazarus"), founded in 1572.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

In addition to these, Vittorio Emanuele claims sovereignty over two more orders:

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Ordine_Civile_di_Savoia_BAR.svg/120px-Ordine_Civile_di_Savoia_BAR.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ordine_Civile_di_Savoia_BAR.svg) [Civil Order of Savoy](/wiki/Civil_Order_of_Savoy "Civil Order of Savoy"), founded in 1831.[[92]](#cite_note-92)
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Cavaliere_OCI_BAR.svg/120px-Cavaliere_OCI_BAR.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cavaliere_OCI_BAR.svg) [Order of the Crown of Italy](/wiki/Order_of_the_Crown_of_Italy "Order of the Crown of Italy"), founded in 1868 and no longer bestowed;[[93]](#cite_note-93) it was replaced by the [Order of Merit of Savoy](/wiki/Order_of_Merit_of_Savoy "Order of Merit of Savoy") in 1988.

In February 2006, all three of [Vittorio Emanuele](/wiki/Vittorio_Emanuele,_Prince_of_Naples "Vittorio Emanuele, Prince of Naples")'s sisters ([Princess Maria Pia](/wiki/Princess_Maria_Pia_of_Bourbon-Parma "Princess Maria Pia of Bourbon-Parma"), [Princess Maria Gabriella](/wiki/Princess_Maria_Gabriella_of_Savoy "Princess Maria Gabriella of Savoy"), and [Princess Maria Beatrice](/wiki/Princess_Maria_Beatrice_of_Savoy "Princess Maria Beatrice of Savoy")) resigned from the Supreme Order of the Most Holy Annunciation and the Order of Saints Maurice and Lazarus, alleging that memberships in the orders had been sold to unworthy candidates, a newfound practice they could not abide.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Cyprus, Jerusalem, and Armenia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Savoy&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Cyprus, Jerusalem, and Armenia")]

In 1396, the title and privileges of the final king of the [Armenian Kingdom of Cilicia](/wiki/Armenian_Kingdom_of_Cilicia "Armenian Kingdom of Cilicia"), [Levon V](/wiki/Levon_V_of_Armenia "Levon V of Armenia"), were transferred to [James I](/wiki/James_I_of_Cyprus "James I of Cyprus"), his cousin and king of Cyprus. The title of King of Armenia was thus united with the titles of King of Cyprus and King of Jerusalem.[[95]](#cite_note-95) The title was held to the modern day by the House of Savoy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Titles of the Crown of Sardinia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Savoy&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Titles of the Crown of Sardinia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/SardiniePiemont.jpg/330px-SardiniePiemont.jpg)](/wiki/File:SardiniePiemont.jpg)

A map of the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)")

The titles of the Crown of Sardinia were the following: "VITTORIO AMEDEO III, per la grazia di Dio Re di Sardegna, Cipro, Gerusalemme e Armenia; Duca di Savoia, Monferrato, Chablais, Aosta e Genevese; Principe di Piemonte ed Oneglia; Marchese in Italia, di Saluzzo, Susa, Ivrea, Ceva, Maro, Oristano, Sezana; Conte di Moriana, Nizza, Tenda, Asti, Alessandria, Goceano; Barone di Vaud e di Faucigny; Signore di Vercelli, Pinerolo, Tarantasia, Lumellino, Val di Sesia; Principe e Vicario perpetuo del Sacro Romano Impero in Italia." The English translation is: "[Victor Amadeus III](/wiki/Victor_Amadeus_III "Victor Amadeus III"), [by the Grace of God](/wiki/By_the_Grace_of_God "By the Grace of God"), [King of Sardinia](/wiki/King_of_Sardinia "King of Sardinia"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cyprus "Kingdom of Cyprus"), [Jerusalem](/wiki/King_of_Jerusalem "King of Jerusalem"), [Armenia](/wiki/Armenian_Kingdom_of_Cilicia "Armenian Kingdom of Cilicia"), [Duke of Savoy](/wiki/Duke_of_Savoy "Duke of Savoy"), [Montferrat](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Montferrat "List of rulers of Montferrat"), [Chablais](/wiki/Chablais "Chablais"), [Aosta](/wiki/Duke_of_Aosta "Duke of Aosta"), and [Genevois](/wiki/Genevois_(province) "Genevois (province)"), [Prince of Piedmont](/wiki/Prince_of_Piedmont "Prince of Piedmont") and [Oneglia](/wiki/Oneglia "Oneglia"), [Marquis](/wiki/Marquis "Marquis") (of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire")) in Italy, of [Saluzzo](/wiki/Saluzzo "Saluzzo"), [Susa](/wiki/March_of_Turin "March of Turin"), [Ivrea](/wiki/Ivrea "Ivrea"), [Ceva](/wiki/Ceva "Ceva"), Maro, [Oristano](/wiki/Oristano "Oristano"), [Sezana](/wiki/Cesana_Torinese "Cesana Torinese"), [Count of Maurienne](/wiki/Count_of_Maurienne "Count of Maurienne"), [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice"), [Tende](/wiki/Tende "Tende"), [Asti](/wiki/Asti "Asti"), [Alessandria](/wiki/Alessandria "Alessandria"), [Goceano](/wiki/Count_of_Goceano "Count of Goceano"), [Baron](/wiki/Baron "Baron") of [Vaud](/wiki/Vaud "Vaud") and [Faucigny](/wiki/Faucigny "Faucigny"), [Lord](/wiki/Lord "Lord") of [Vercelli](/wiki/Vercelli "Vercelli"), [Pinerolo](/wiki/Pinerolo "Pinerolo"), [Tarentaise](/wiki/Mo%C3%BBtiers "Moûtiers"), [Lumellino](/wiki/Lomellina "Lomellina"), [Val di Sesia](/wiki/Valsesia "Valsesia"), [and] Prince and perpetual [Vicar](/wiki/Vicar "Vicar") of the Holy Roman Empire in Italy."
